[Hypnagogia](/wiki/Hypnagogia "Hypnagogia"), [Peduncular hallucinosis](/wiki/Peduncular_hallucinosis "Peduncular hallucinosis"), [Delirium tremens](/wiki/Delirium_tremens "Delirium tremens"), [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"), [Delusion](/wiki/Delusion "Delusion"), [Lewy body dementia](/wiki/Dementia_with_Lewy_bodies "Dementia with Lewy bodies"), [Charles Bonnet syndrome](/wiki/Visual_release_hallucinations "Visual release hallucinations"), [hallucinogens](/wiki/Hallucinogen "Hallucinogen"), [sensory deprivation](/wiki/Sensory_deprivation "Sensory deprivation"), [schizophrenia](/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia"), [psychedelics](/wiki/Psychedelics "Psychedelics"), [sleep paralysis](/wiki/Sleep_paralysis "Sleep paralysis"), [drug intoxication](/wiki/Drug_intoxication "Drug intoxication") or [withdrawal](/wiki/Drug_withdrawal "Drug withdrawal"), [sleep deprivation](/wiki/Sleep_deprivation "Sleep deprivation"), [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy"), [psychological stress](/wiki/Psychological_stress "Psychological stress"), [rabies](/wiki/Rabies "Rabies"), [non-celiac gluten sensitivity](/wiki/Non-celiac_gluten_sensitivity "Non-celiac gluten sensitivity"), [fever](/wiki/Fever "Fever"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) [covert](/wiki/Covert "Covert") [weaponry](/wiki/Weaponry "Weaponry")[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-theconversation.com-3)

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Hallucination%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Hallucination%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Hallucination%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Hallucination%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(May 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

is a [perception](/wiki/Perception "Perception") in the absence of an external context [stimulus](/wiki/Stimulus_(physiology) "Stimulus (physiology)") that has the compelling sense of reality.[[6]](#cite_note-6) They are distinguishable from several related [phenomena](/wiki/Phenomena "Phenomena"), such as dreaming ([REM sleep](/wiki/Rapid_eye_movement_sleep "Rapid eye movement sleep")), which does not involve wakefulness; [pseudohallucination](/wiki/Pseudohallucination "Pseudohallucination"), which does not mimic real perception, and is accurately perceived as unreal; [illusion](/wiki/Illusion "Illusion"), which involves distorted or misinterpreted real perception; and [mental imagery](/wiki/Mental_imagery "Mental imagery"), which does not mimic real perception, and is under voluntary control.[[7]](#cite_note-Chiu-7) Hallucinations also differ from "[delusional](/wiki/Delusion "Delusion") perceptions", in which a correctly sensed and interpreted stimulus (i.e., a real perception) is given some additional significance.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Perception that only seems real

For other uses, see [Hallucination (disambiguation)](/wiki/Hallucination_(disambiguation) "Hallucination (disambiguation)").

A **hallucination** is a [perception](/wiki/Perception "Perception") in the absence of an external context [stimulus](/wiki/Stimulus_(physiology) "Stimulus (physiology)") that has the compelling sense of reality.[[6]](#cite_note-6) They are distinguishable from several related [phenomena](/wiki/Phenomena "Phenomena"), such as dreaming ([REM sleep](/wiki/Rapid_eye_movement_sleep "Rapid eye movement sleep")), which does not involve wakefulness; [pseudohallucination](/wiki/Pseudohallucination "Pseudohallucination"), which does not mimic real perception, and is accurately perceived as unreal; [illusion](/wiki/Illusion "Illusion"), which involves distorted or misinterpreted real perception; and [mental imagery](/wiki/Mental_imagery "Mental imagery"), which does not mimic real perception, and is under voluntary control.[[7]](#cite_note-Chiu-7) Hallucinations also differ from "[delusional](/wiki/Delusion "Delusion") perceptions", in which a correctly sensed and interpreted stimulus (i.e., a real perception) is given some additional significance.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Hallucinations can occur in any [sensory modality](/wiki/Stimulus_modality "Stimulus modality")—[visual](/wiki/Visual_system "Visual system"), [auditory](/wiki/Auditory_hallucination "Auditory hallucination"), [olfactory](/wiki/Olfaction "Olfaction"), [gustatory](/wiki/Taste "Taste"), [tactile](/wiki/Tactition "Tactition"), [proprioceptive](/wiki/Proprioception "Proprioception"), [equilibrioceptive](/wiki/Equilibrioception "Equilibrioception"), [nociceptive](/wiki/Nociception "Nociception"), [thermoceptive](/wiki/Thermoception "Thermoception") and [chronoceptive](/wiki/Time_perception "Time perception"). Hallucinations are referred to as multimodal if multiple sensory modalities occur.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10)

A mild form of hallucination is known as a *disturbance*, and can occur in most of the senses above. These may be things like seeing movement in [peripheral vision](/wiki/Peripheral_vision "Peripheral vision"), or hearing faint noises or voices. Auditory hallucinations are very common in [schizophrenia](/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia"). They may be benevolent (telling the subject good things about themselves) or malicious (cursing the subject). 55% of auditory hallucinations are malicious in content,[[11]](#cite_note-11) for example, people talking about the subject, not speaking to them directly. Like auditory hallucinations, the source of the visual counterpart can also be behind the subject. This can produce a feeling of being looked or stared at, usually with malicious intent.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Frequently, auditory hallucinations and their visual counterpart are experienced by the subject together.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[Hypnagogic hallucinations](/wiki/Hypnagogic_hallucination "Hypnagogic hallucination") and [hypnopompic hallucinations](/wiki/Hypnopompia "Hypnopompia") are considered normal phenomena. Hypnagogic hallucinations can occur as one is falling asleep and hypnopompic hallucinations occur when one is waking up. Hallucinations can be associated with [drug](/wiki/Psychoactive_drug "Psychoactive drug") use (particularly [deliriants](/wiki/Deliriant "Deliriant")), [sleep deprivation](/wiki/Sleep_deprivation "Sleep deprivation"), [psychosis](/wiki/Psychosis "Psychosis") (including stress-related psychosis[[15]](#cite_note-15)), [neurological disorders](/wiki/Neurological_disorder "Neurological disorder"), and [delirium tremens](/wiki/Delirium_tremens "Delirium tremens"). Many hallucinations happen also during [sleep paralysis](/wiki/Sleep_paralysis "Sleep paralysis").[[16]](#cite_note-newrev2018-16)

The word "hallucination" itself was introduced into the English language by the 17th-century physician Sir [Thomas Browne](/wiki/Thomas_Browne "Thomas Browne") in 1646 from the derivation of the Latin word *alucinari* meaning to wander in the mind. For Browne, hallucination means a sort of vision that is "depraved and receive[s] its objects erroneously".[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Classification")]

Hallucinations may be manifested in a variety of forms.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Various forms of hallucinations affect different senses, sometimes occurring simultaneously, creating multiple sensory hallucinations for those experiencing them.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)

### Auditory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Auditory")]

Main article: [Auditory hallucination](/wiki/Auditory_hallucination "Auditory hallucination")

[Auditory](/wiki/Sound "Sound") hallucinations (also known as *paracusia*)[[19]](#cite_note-19) are the perception of sound without outside stimulus. Auditory hallucinations can be divided into elementary and complex, along with verbal and nonverbal. These hallucinations are the most common type of hallucination, with auditory verbal hallucinations being more common than nonverbal.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-:5-21) Elementary hallucinations are the perception of sounds such as hissing, whistling, an extended tone, and more.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In many cases, [tinnitus](/wiki/Tinnitus "Tinnitus") is an elementary auditory hallucination.[[21]](#cite_note-:5-21) However, some people who experience certain types of tinnitus, especially pulsatile tinnitus, are actually hearing the blood rushing through vessels near the ear. Because the auditory stimulus is present in this situation, it does not qualify it as a hallucination.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Complex hallucinations are those of voices, music,[[21]](#cite_note-:5-21) or other sounds that may or may not be clear, may or may not be familiar, and may be friendly, aggressive, or among other possibilities. A hallucination of a single individual person of one or more talking voices is particularly associated with [psychotic](/wiki/Psychosis "Psychosis") disorders such as [schizophrenia](/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia"), and hold special significance in diagnosing these conditions.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In schizophrenia, voices are normally perceived coming from outside the person, but in dissociative disorders they are perceived as originating from within the person, commenting in their head instead of behind their back. Differential diagnosis between schizophrenia and [dissociative disorders](/wiki/Dissociative_disorder "Dissociative disorder") is challenging due to many overlapping symptoms, especially [Schneiderian first rank symptoms](/wiki/Kurt_Schneider "Kurt Schneider") such as hallucinations.[[25]](#cite_note-25) However, many people who do not have a diagnosable [mental illness](/wiki/Mental_illness "Mental illness") may sometimes hear voices as well.[[26]](#cite_note-26) One important example to consider when forming a differential diagnosis for a patient with paracusia is lateral [temporal lobe epilepsy](/wiki/Temporal_lobe_epilepsy "Temporal lobe epilepsy"). Despite the tendency to associate hearing voices, or otherwise hallucinating, and [psychosis](/wiki/Psychosis "Psychosis") with schizophrenia or other psychiatric illnesses, it is crucial to take into consideration that, even if a person does exhibit psychotic features, they do not necessarily have a psychiatric disorder on its own. Disorders such as [Wilson's disease](/wiki/Wilson%27s_disease "Wilson's disease"), various [endocrine diseases](/wiki/Endocrine_disease "Endocrine disease"), numerous [metabolic disturbances](/wiki/Metabolic_disturbance "Metabolic disturbance"), [multiple sclerosis](/wiki/Multiple_sclerosis "Multiple sclerosis"), [systemic lupus erythematosus](/wiki/Systemic_lupus_erythematosus "Systemic lupus erythematosus"), [porphyria](/wiki/Porphyria "Porphyria"), [sarcoidosis](/wiki/Sarcoidosis "Sarcoidosis"), and many others can present with psychosis.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Musical hallucinations are also relatively common in terms of complex auditory hallucinations and may be the result of a wide range of causes ranging from hearing-loss (such as in [musical ear syndrome](/wiki/Musical_ear_syndrome "Musical ear syndrome"), the auditory version of [Charles Bonnet syndrome](/wiki/Charles_Bonnet_syndrome "Charles Bonnet syndrome")), lateral temporal lobe epilepsy,[[28]](#cite_note-28) arteriovenous malformation,[[29]](#cite_note-pmid19682829-29) stroke, [lesion](/wiki/Lesion "Lesion"), [abscess](/wiki/Abscess "Abscess"), or tumor.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The [Hearing Voices Movement](/wiki/Hearing_Voices_Movement "Hearing Voices Movement") is a support and advocacy group for people who hallucinate voices, but do not otherwise show signs of mental illness or impairment.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

High [caffeine](/wiki/Caffeine "Caffeine") consumption has been linked to an increase in likelihood of one experiencing auditory hallucinations.[[32]](#cite_note-32) A study conducted by the [La Trobe University](/wiki/La_Trobe_University "La Trobe University") School of Psychological Sciences revealed that as few as five cups of coffee a day (approximately 500 mg of caffeine) could trigger the phenomenon.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Visual

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Visual")]

Main article: [Visual hallucination](/wiki/Visual_hallucination "Visual hallucination")

See also: [Pareidolia](/wiki/Pareidolia "Pareidolia") and [Palinopsia](/wiki/Palinopsia "Palinopsia")

A *visual hallucination* is "the perception of an external visual stimulus where none exists".[[34]](#cite_note-34) A separate but related phenomenon is a *visual illusion*, which is a distortion of a real external stimulus. Visual hallucinations are classified as simple or complex:

* *Simple visual hallucinations* (SVH) are also referred to as non-formed visual hallucinations and elementary visual hallucinations. These terms refer to lights, colors, geometric shapes, and indiscrete objects. These can be further subdivided into [phosphenes](/wiki/Phosphene "Phosphene") which are SVH without structure, and [photopsias](/wiki/Photopsia "Photopsia") which are SVH with geometric structures.
* *Complex visual hallucinations* (CVH) are also referred to as formed visual hallucinations. CVHs are clear, lifelike images or scenes such as people, animals, objects, places, etc.

For example, one may report hallucinating a giraffe. A simple visual hallucination is an amorphous figure that may have a similar shape or color to a giraffe (*looks like* a giraffe), while a complex visual hallucination is a discrete, lifelike image that *is*, unmistakably, a giraffe.

### Command

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Command")]

See also: [Bicameral mentality](/wiki/Bicameral_mentality "Bicameral mentality")

Command hallucinations are hallucinations in the form of commands; they appear to be from an external source, or can appear coming from the subject's head.[[35]](#cite_note-Beck-Sander1997-35) The contents of the hallucinations can range from the innocuous to commands to cause harm to the self or others.[[35]](#cite_note-Beck-Sander1997-35) Command hallucinations are often associated with [schizophrenia](/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia"). People experiencing command hallucinations may or may not comply with the hallucinated commands, depending on the circumstances. Compliance is more common for non-violent commands.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Command hallucinations are sometimes used to defend a crime that has been committed, often homicides.[[37]](#cite_note-37) In essence, it is a voice that one hears and it tells the listener what to do. Sometimes the commands are quite benign directives such as "Stand up" or "Shut the door."[[38]](#cite_note-Shea-38) Whether it is a command for something simple or something that is a threat, it is still considered a "command hallucination." Some helpful questions that can assist one in determining if they may have this includes: "What are the voices telling you to do?", "When did your voices first start telling you to do things?", "Do you recognize the person who is telling you to harm yourself (or others)?", "Do you think you can resist doing what the voices are telling you to do?"[[38]](#cite_note-Shea-38)

### Olfactory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Olfactory")]

Main articles: [Phantosmia](/wiki/Phantosmia "Phantosmia") and [Parosmia](/wiki/Parosmia "Parosmia")

Phantosmia (olfactory hallucinations), smelling an odor that is not actually there,[[39]](#cite_note-NHSphantosmia-39) and parosmia (olfactory illusions), inhaling a real odor but perceiving it as different scent than remembered,[[40]](#cite_note-Hong-40) are distortions to the sense of smell ([olfactory system](/wiki/Olfactory_system "Olfactory system")), and in most cases, are not caused by anything serious and will usually go away on their own in time.[[39]](#cite_note-NHSphantosmia-39) It can result from a range of conditions such as nasal infections, [nasal polyps](/wiki/Nasal_polyp "Nasal polyp"), dental problems, migraines, head injuries, [seizures](/wiki/Seizure "Seizure"), strokes, or brain tumors.[[39]](#cite_note-NHSphantosmia-39)[[41]](#cite_note-Leopold2002-41) Environmental exposures can sometimes cause it as well, such as smoking, exposure to certain types of chemicals (e.g., [insecticides](/wiki/Insecticide "Insecticide") or [solvents](/wiki/Solvent "Solvent")), or radiation treatment for head or neck cancer.[[39]](#cite_note-NHSphantosmia-39) It can also be a symptom of certain [mental disorders](/wiki/Mental_disorder "Mental disorder") such as [depression](/wiki/Major_depression "Major depression"), [bipolar disorder](/wiki/Bipolar_disorder "Bipolar disorder"), intoxication, substance [withdrawal](/wiki/Drug_withdrawal "Drug withdrawal"), or [psychotic disorders](/wiki/Psychotic_disorder "Psychotic disorder") (e.g., [schizophrenia](/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia")).[[41]](#cite_note-Leopold2002-41) The perceived odors are usually unpleasant and commonly described as smelling burned, foul, spoiled, or rotten.[[39]](#cite_note-NHSphantosmia-39)

### Tactile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Tactile")]

Main article: [Tactile hallucination](/wiki/Tactile_hallucination "Tactile hallucination")

Tactile hallucinations are the illusion of tactile sensory input, simulating various types of pressure to the skin or other organs. One subtype of tactile hallucination, [formication](/wiki/Formication "Formication"), is the sensation of insects crawling underneath the skin and is frequently associated with prolonged [cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine "Cocaine") use.[[42]](#cite_note-Berrios_1982-42) However, formication may also be the result of normal hormonal changes such as [menopause](/wiki/Menopause "Menopause"), or disorders such as [peripheral neuropathy](/wiki/Peripheral_neuropathy "Peripheral neuropathy"), high fevers, [Lyme disease](/wiki/Lyme_disease "Lyme disease"), [skin cancer](/wiki/Skin_cancer "Skin cancer"), and more.[[42]](#cite_note-Berrios_1982-42)

### Gustatory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Gustatory")]

This type of hallucination is the perception of taste without a stimulus. These hallucinations, which are typically strange or unpleasant, are relatively common among individuals who have certain types of [focal epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy"), especially [temporal lobe epilepsy](/wiki/Temporal_lobe_epilepsy "Temporal lobe epilepsy"). The regions of the brain responsible for gustatory hallucination in this case are the [insula](/wiki/Insular_cortex "Insular cortex") and the superior bank of the [sylvian fissure](/wiki/Sylvian_fissure "Sylvian fissure").[[43]](#cite_note-Panayiotopoulos_2007-43)[[44]](#cite_note-Barker_1997-44)

### Sexual

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sexual")]

Sexual hallucinations are the perception of [erogenous](/wiki/Erogenous_zone "Erogenous zone") or orgasmic stimuli. They may be unimodal or multimodal in nature and frequently involve sensation in the genital region, though it is not exclusive.[[45]](#cite_note-:6-45) Frequent examples of sexual hallucinations include the sensation of being penetrated, experiencing orgasm, feeling as if one is being touched in an erogenous zone, sensing stimulation in the genitals, feeling the fondling of one's [breasts](/wiki/Breast "Breast") or [buttocks](/wiki/Buttocks "Buttocks") and tastes or smells related to sexual activity.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Visualizations of sexual content and auditory voices making [sexually violence](/wiki/Sexual_violence "Sexual violence") remarks may sometimes be included in this classification. While it features components of other classifications, sexual hallucinations are distinct due to the orgasmic component and unique presentation.[[47]](#cite_note-:7-47)

The regions of the brain responsible differ by the subsection of sexual hallucination. In orgasmic auras, the mesial [temporal lobe](/wiki/Temporal_lobe "Temporal lobe"), right [amygdala](/wiki/Amygdala "Amygdala") and [hippocampus](/wiki/Hippocampus "Hippocampus") are involved.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49) In males, genital specific sensations are related to the [postcentral gyrus](/wiki/Postcentral_gyrus "Postcentral gyrus") and arousal and ejaculation are linked to stimulation in the [posterior frontal lobe](/wiki/Frontal_lobe "Frontal lobe").[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-:8-51) In females, however, the [hippocampus](/wiki/Hippocampus "Hippocampus") and [amygdala](/wiki/Amygdala "Amygdala") are connected.[[51]](#cite_note-:8-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) Limited studies have been done to understand the mechanism of action behind sexual hallucinations in [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy"), [substance use](/wiki/Substance_abuse "Substance abuse"), and [post-traumatic stress disorder](/wiki/Post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Post-traumatic stress disorder") etiologies.[[47]](#cite_note-:7-47)

### Somatic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Somatic")]

Somatic hallucinations refer to an interoceptive sensory experience in the absence of stimulus. Somatic hallucinations can be broken down into further subcategories: general, algesic, kinesthetic, and cenesthopathic.[[45]](#cite_note-:6-45)[[47]](#cite_note-:7-47)

* **Cenesthopathic**- Effecting the [cenesthetic](/wiki/Cenesthopathy "Cenesthopathy") [sensory modality](/wiki/Sensory_modality "Sensory modality"), cenesthopathic hallucinations are a pathological alteration in the sense of bodily existence, caused by aberrant bodily sensations. Most often, cenesthopathic hallucinations will refer to sensation in the visceral organs. Therefore, it is also known as visceral hallucinations.[[53]](#cite_note-:9-53)[[47]](#cite_note-:7-47) Manifestations are often subjective, hard to describe and unique to the sufferer. Common manifestations include pressure, burning, tickling, or tightening in various body systems.[[54]](#cite_note-:10-54) While these hallucinations can be experienced by a variety of psychiatric and neurological disorder, cenesthopathic schizophrenia is recognized by the [ICD](/wiki/International_Classification_of_Diseases "International Classification of Diseases") as a subtype of [schizophrenia](/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia") marked by primarily cenesthopathic hallucinations and other [body image aberrations](/wiki/Body_image_disturbance "Body image disturbance").[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[47]](#cite_note-:7-47)
* **Kinesthetic-** Kinesthetic hallucinations, effecting the [sensory modality](/wiki/Stimulus_modality "Stimulus modality") of [the same name](/wiki/Proprioception "Proprioception"), are the sensation of movement of the limbs or other body parts without actual movement.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[47]](#cite_note-:7-47)[[54]](#cite_note-:10-54)[[53]](#cite_note-:9-53)
* **Algesic-** Algesic hallucinations, effecting the [algesic](/wiki/Pain "Pain") [sensory modality](/wiki/Stimulus_modality "Stimulus modality"), refers to a perceived perception of pain.[[47]](#cite_note-:7-47)[[54]](#cite_note-:10-54)[[53]](#cite_note-:9-53)
* **General-** General [somatic](/wiki/Somatics "Somatics") hallucination refers to somatic hallucinations not otherwise categorized by the above subsections. Common examples include when an individual feels that their body is being mutilated, i.e. twisted, torn, or disemboweled. Other reported cases are invasion by animals in the person's internal organs, such as snakes in the stomach or frogs in the [rectum](/wiki/Rectum "Rectum"). The general feeling that one's flesh is decomposing is also classified under this type of this hallucination.[[47]](#cite_note-:7-47)

### Multimodal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Multimodal")]

A hallucination involving [sensory modalities](/wiki/Stimulus_modality "Stimulus modality") is called multimodal, analogous to unimodal hallucinations which have only one sensory modality. The multiple sensory modalities can occur at the same time (simultaneously) or with a delay (serial), be related or unrelated to each other, and be consistent with reality (congruent) or not (incongruent).[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10) For example, a person talking in a hallucination would be congruent with reality, but a cat talking would not be.

Multimodal hallucinations are correlated to poorer mental health outcomes, and are often experienced as feeling more real.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)

## Cause

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Cause")]

See also: [Psychosis (causes)](/wiki/Psychosis#Causes "Psychosis")

Hallucinations can be caused by a number of factors.[[3]](#cite_note-theconversation.com-3)

### Hypnagogic hallucination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Hypnagogic hallucination")]

Main article: [Hypnagogia](/wiki/Hypnagogia "Hypnagogia")

These hallucinations occur just before falling asleep and affect a high proportion of the population: in one survey 37% of the respondents experienced them twice a week.[[57]](#cite_note-Ohayon-57) The hallucinations can last from seconds to minutes; all the while, the subject usually remains aware of the true nature of the images. These may be associated with [narcolepsy](/wiki/Narcolepsy "Narcolepsy"). Hypnagogic hallucinations are sometimes associated with brainstem abnormalities, but this is rare.[[58]](#cite_note-manford-58)

### Peduncular hallucinosis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Peduncular hallucinosis")]

Main article: [Peduncular hallucinosis](/wiki/Peduncular_hallucinosis "Peduncular hallucinosis")

Peduncular means pertaining to the [peduncle](/wiki/Cerebral_peduncle "Cerebral peduncle"), which is a neural tract running to and from the [pons](/wiki/Pons "Pons") on the [brain stem](/wiki/Brain_stem "Brain stem"). These hallucinations usually occur in the evenings, but not during drowsiness, as in the case of hypnagogic hallucination. The subject is usually fully conscious and then can interact with the hallucinatory characters for extended periods of time. As in the case of [hypnagogic hallucinations](/wiki/Hypnagogic_hallucination "Hypnagogic hallucination"), insight into the nature of the images remains intact. The false images can occur in any part of the visual field, and are rarely [polymodal](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/polymodal "wikt:polymodal").[[58]](#cite_note-manford-58)

### Delirium tremens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Delirium tremens")]

Main article: [Delirium tremens](/wiki/Delirium_tremens "Delirium tremens")

One of the more enigmatic forms of visual hallucination is the highly variable, possibly polymodal [delirium tremens](/wiki/Delirium_tremens "Delirium tremens"). It is associated with withdrawal in [alcohol use disorder](/wiki/Alcohol_use_disorder "Alcohol use disorder"). Individuals with delirium tremens may be agitated and confused, especially in the later stages of this disease.[[59]](#cite_note-59) Insight is gradually reduced with the progression of this disorder. Sleep is disturbed and occurs for a shorter period of time, with [rapid eye movement sleep](/wiki/Rapid_eye_movement_sleep "Rapid eye movement sleep").[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### Parkinson's disease and Lewy body dementia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Parkinson's disease and Lewy body dementia")]

[Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease") is linked with [Lewy body dementia](/wiki/Dementia_with_Lewy_bodies "Dementia with Lewy bodies") for their similar hallucinatory symptoms. Presence hallucinations can be an early indicator of cognitive decline in Parkinson's Disease.[[61]](#cite_note-61) The symptoms strike during the evening in any part of the visual field, and are rarely [polymodal](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/polymodal "wikt:polymodal"). The segue into hallucination may begin with illusions[[62]](#cite_note-62) where sensory perception is greatly distorted, but no novel sensory information is present. These typically last for several minutes, during which time the subject may be either conscious and normal or drowsy/inaccessible. Insight into these hallucinations is usually preserved and [REM sleep](/wiki/REM_sleep "REM sleep") is usually reduced. Parkinson's disease is usually associated with a degraded [substantia nigra](/wiki/Substantia_nigra "Substantia nigra") pars compacta, but recent evidence suggests that PD affects a number of sites in the brain. Some places of noted degradation include the median [raphe nuclei](/wiki/Raphe_nuclei "Raphe nuclei"), the [noradrenergic](/wiki/Noradrenergic "Noradrenergic") parts of the [locus coeruleus](/wiki/Locus_coeruleus "Locus coeruleus"), and the [cholinergic](/wiki/Cholinergic "Cholinergic") neurons in the [parabrachial area](/wiki/Parabrachial_area "Parabrachial area") and [pedunculopontine](/wiki/Pedunculopontine "Pedunculopontine") nuclei of the [tegmentum](/wiki/Tegmentum "Tegmentum").[[58]](#cite_note-manford-58)

### Migraine coma

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Migraine coma")]

This type of hallucination is usually experienced during the recovery from a comatose state. The migraine coma can last for up to two days, and a state of depression is sometimes [comorbid](/wiki/Comorbidity "Comorbidity"). The hallucinations occur during states of full consciousness, and insight into the hallucinatory nature of the images is preserved. It has been noted that ataxic lesions accompany the migraine coma.[[58]](#cite_note-manford-58)

#### Migraine attacks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Migraine attacks")]

See also: [Migraine aura](/wiki/Migraine_aura "Migraine aura") and [Scintillating scotoma](/wiki/Scintillating_scotoma "Scintillating scotoma")

Migraine attacks may result in visual hallucinations including auras and in rarer cases, auditory hallucinations.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### Focal epilepsy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Focal epilepsy")]

Visual hallucinations due to [focal seizures](/wiki/Focal_seizure "Focal seizure") differ depending on the region of the brain where the seizure occurs. For example, visual hallucinations during [occipital lobe](/wiki/Occipital_lobe "Occipital lobe") seizures are typically visions of brightly colored, geometric shapes that may move across the [visual field](/wiki/Visual_field "Visual field"), multiply, or form concentric rings and generally persist from a few seconds to a few minutes. They are usually unilateral and localized to one part of the visual field on the contralateral side of the seizure focus, typically the [temporal field](/wiki/Temporal_lobe "Temporal lobe"). However, unilateral visions moving horizontally across the [visual field](/wiki/Visual_field "Visual field") begin on the contralateral side and move toward the ipsilateral side.[[43]](#cite_note-Panayiotopoulos_2007-43)[[64]](#cite_note-Teeple_2009-64)

[Temporal lobe seizures](/wiki/Temporal_lobe_epilepsy "Temporal lobe epilepsy"), on the other hand, can produce complex visual hallucinations of people, scenes, animals, and more as well as distortions of [visual perception](/wiki/Visual_perception "Visual perception"). Complex hallucinations may appear to be real or unreal, may or may not be distorted with respect to size, and may seem disturbing or affable, among other variables. One rare but notable type of hallucination is [heautoscopy](/wiki/Heautoscopy "Heautoscopy"), a hallucination of a mirror image of one's self. These "other selves" may be perfectly still or performing complex tasks, may be an image of a younger self or the present self, and tend to be briefly present. Complex hallucinations are a relatively uncommon finding in temporal lobe epilepsy patients. Rarely, they may occur during occipital focal seizures or in [parietal lobe](/wiki/Parietal_lobe "Parietal lobe") seizures.[[43]](#cite_note-Panayiotopoulos_2007-43)

Distortions in visual perception during a temporal lobe seizure may include size distortion ([micropsia](/wiki/Micropsia "Micropsia") or [macropsia](/wiki/Macropsia "Macropsia")), distorted perception of movement (where moving objects may appear to be moving very slowly or to be perfectly still), a sense that surfaces such as ceilings and even entire horizons are moving farther away in a fashion similar to the [dolly zoom effect](/wiki/Dolly_zoom "Dolly zoom"), and other illusions.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Even when consciousness is impaired, insight into the hallucination or illusion is typically preserved.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

### Drug-induced hallucination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Drug-induced hallucination")]

Main article: [Hallucinogen](/wiki/Hallucinogen "Hallucinogen")

Drug-induced hallucinations are caused by [hallucinogens](/wiki/Hallucinogen "Hallucinogen"), [dissociatives](/wiki/Dissociative "Dissociative"), and [deliriants](/wiki/Deliriants "Deliriants"), including many drugs with [anticholinergic](/wiki/Anticholinergic "Anticholinergic") actions and certain stimulants, which are known to cause visual and auditory hallucinations. Some psychedelics such as [lysergic acid diethylamide](/wiki/Lysergic_acid_diethylamide "Lysergic acid diethylamide") (LSD) and [psilocybin](/wiki/Psilocybin "Psilocybin") can cause hallucinations that range in the spectrum of mild to intense.[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Hallucinations, [pseudohallucinations](/wiki/Pseudohallucination "Pseudohallucination"), or intensification of [pareidolia](/wiki/Pareidolia "Pareidolia"), particularly auditory, are known side effects of [opioids](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid") to different degrees—it may be associated with the absolute degree of agonism or antagonism of especially the [kappa opioid receptor](/wiki/Kappa_opioid_receptor "Kappa opioid receptor"), [sigma receptors](/wiki/Sigma_receptor "Sigma receptor"), [delta opioid receptor](/wiki/Delta_opioid_receptor "Delta opioid receptor") and the [NMDA receptors](/wiki/NMDA_receptor "NMDA receptor") or the overall receptor activation profile as synthetic opioids like those of the [pentazocine](/wiki/Pentazocine "Pentazocine"), [levorphanol](/wiki/Levorphanol "Levorphanol"), [fentanyl](/wiki/Fentanyl "Fentanyl"), [pethidine](/wiki/Pethidine "Pethidine"), [methadone](/wiki/Methadone "Methadone") and some other families are more associated with this side effect than natural opioids like [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") and [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine") and semi-synthetics like [hydromorphone](/wiki/Hydromorphone "Hydromorphone"), amongst which there also appears to be a stronger correlation with the relative analgesic strength. Three opioids, [Cyclazocine](/wiki/Cyclazocine "Cyclazocine") (a benzormorphan opioid/pentazocine relative) and two levorphanol-related [morphinan](/wiki/Morphinan "Morphinan") opioids, [Cyclorphan](/wiki/Cyclorphan "Cyclorphan") and Dextrorphan are classified as hallucinogens, and [Dextromethorphan](/wiki/Dextromethorphan "Dextromethorphan") as a dissociative.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72) These drugs also can induce sleep (relating to hypnagogic hallucinations) and especially the pethidines have atropine-like [anticholinergic](/wiki/Anticholinergic "Anticholinergic") activity, which was possibly also a limiting factor in the use, the psychotomimetic side effects of potentiating morphine, [oxycodone](/wiki/Oxycodone "Oxycodone"), and other opioids with [scopolamine](/wiki/Scopolamine "Scopolamine") (respectively in the Twilight Sleep technique and the combination drug Skophedal, which was eukodal (oxycodone), scopolamine and [ephedrine](/wiki/Ephedrine "Ephedrine"), called the "wonder drug of the 1930s" after its invention in Germany in 1928, but only rarely specially compounded today) (q.q.v.).[[73]](#cite_note-73)

### Sensory deprivation hallucination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Sensory deprivation hallucination")]

Hallucinations can be caused by [sensory deprivation](/wiki/Sensory_deprivation "Sensory deprivation") when it occurs for prolonged periods of time, and almost always occurs in the modality being deprived (visual for blindfolded/darkness, auditory for muffled conditions, etc.).[[74]](#cite_note-74) Though, some researchers found that in some people hallucinations can be induced after just 15 minutes of sensory deprivation.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Research on hallucinations after involuntary sensory deprivation - such as in the cases of [vision](/wiki/Visual_impairment "Visual impairment") ([Charles Bonnet syndrome](/wiki/Charles_Bonnet_syndrome "Charles Bonnet syndrome")) or [hearing loss](/wiki/Hearing_loss "Hearing loss") ([Musical ear syndrome](/wiki/Musical_ear_syndrome "Musical ear syndrome"))- hypothesizes that they are caused by the brain's need to receive [sensory input for processing](/wiki/Sensory_processing "Sensory processing"). Loss of sensory input causes sudden neural firings in the regions responsible for processing them. The brain then misattributes the source of these internal [neural firings](/wiki/Neural_firing "Neural firing") as coming from external sensory source, which manifest themselves as hallucinations. Though, no definitive cause for these type of hallucinations has been found. These hallucinations usually resolve or reduce in frequency once sensory input is either restored or increased (e.g. after getting [hearing aids](/wiki/Hearing_aid "Hearing aid")) or after some time has passed and the brain [is able to reorganize itself](/wiki/Neuroplasticity "Neuroplasticity").[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

#### Charles Bonnet syndrome

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Charles Bonnet syndrome")]

[Charles Bonnet syndrome](/wiki/Visual_release_hallucinations "Visual release hallucinations") is the name given to visual hallucinations experienced by a partially or severely [sight impaired](/wiki/Visual_impairment "Visual impairment") person. The hallucinations can occur at any time and can distress people of any age, as they may not initially be aware that they are hallucinating. They may fear for their own mental health initially, which may delay them sharing with carers until they start to understand it themselves. The hallucinations can frighten and disconcert as to what is real and what is not. The hallucinations can sometimes be dispersed by eye movements, or by reasoned logic such as, "I can see fire but there is no smoke and there is no heat from it" or perhaps, "We have an infestation of rats but they have pink ribbons with a bell tied on their necks." Over elapsed months and years, the hallucinations may become more or less frequent with changes in ability to see. The length of time that the sight impaired person can have these hallucinations varies according to the underlying speed of eye deterioration. A differential diagnosis are ophthalmopathic hallucinations.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

### Experimentally-induced hallucinations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Experimentally-induced hallucinations")]

Main article: [Hallucinations in the sane](/wiki/Hallucinations_in_the_sane "Hallucinations in the sane")

**Anomalous experiences**, such as so-called benign hallucinations, may occur in a person in a state of good mental and physical health, even in the apparent absence of a transient trigger factor such as [fatigue](/wiki/Fatigue_(medical) "Fatigue (medical)"), [intoxication](/wiki/Substance_intoxication "Substance intoxication") or [sensory deprivation](/wiki/Sensory_deprivation "Sensory deprivation").

The evidence for this statement has been accumulating for more than a century. Studies of benign hallucinatory experiences go back to 1886 and the early work of the [Society for Psychical Research](/wiki/Society_for_Psychical_Research "Society for Psychical Research"),[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) which suggested approximately 10% of the population had experienced at least one hallucinatory episode in the course of their life. More recent studies have validated these findings; the precise incidence found varies with the nature of the episode and the criteria of "hallucination" adopted, but the basic finding is now well-supported.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

### Non-celiac gluten sensitivity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Non-celiac gluten sensitivity")]

There is tentative evidence of a relationship with [non-celiac gluten sensitivity](/wiki/Non-celiac_gluten_sensitivity "Non-celiac gluten sensitivity"), the so-called "gluten psychosis".[[82]](#cite_note-LosurdoPrincipi2018-82)

## Pathophysiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Pathophysiology")]

### Dopaminergic and serotonergic hallucinations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Dopaminergic and serotonergic hallucinations")]

It has been reported that in serotonergic hallucinations, the person maintains an awareness that they are hallucinating, unlike dopaminergic hallucinations.[[16]](#cite_note-newrev2018-16)

### Neuroanatomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Neuroanatomy")]

Hallucinations are associated with structural and functional abnormalities in primary and secondary sensory cortices. Reduced grey matter in regions of the [superior temporal gyrus](/wiki/Superior_temporal_gyrus "Superior temporal gyrus")/[middle temporal gyrus](/wiki/Middle_temporal_gyrus "Middle temporal gyrus"), including [Broca's area](/wiki/Broca%27s_area "Broca's area"), is associated with auditory hallucinations as a trait, while acute hallucinations are associated with increased activity in the same regions along with the [hippocampus](/wiki/Hippocampus "Hippocampus"), [parahippocampus](/wiki/Parahippocampus "Parahippocampus"), and the right hemispheric homologue of Broca's area in the inferior frontal gyrus.[[83]](#cite_note-Brown-83) Grey and white matter abnormalities in visual regions are associated with hallucinations in diseases such as [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease"), further supporting the notion of dysfunction in sensory regions underlying hallucinations.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

One proposed model of hallucinations posits that over-activity in sensory regions, which is normally attributed to internal sources via feedforward networks to the inferior frontal gyrus, is interpreted as originating externally due to abnormal connectivity or functionality of the feedforward network.[[83]](#cite_note-Brown-83) This is supported by cognitive studies of those with hallucinations, who have demonstrated abnormal attribution of self generated stimuli.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

Disruptions in thalamocortical circuitry may underlie the observed top down and bottom up dysfunction.[[86]](#cite_note-86) Thalamocortical circuits, composed of projections between thalamic and cortical neurons and adjacent interneurons, underlie certain electrophysical characteristics ([gamma oscillations](/wiki/Gamma_oscillations "Gamma oscillations")) that are associated with sensory processing. Cortical inputs to thalamic neurons enable attentional modulation of sensory neurons. Dysfunction in sensory afferents, and abnormal cortical input may result in pre-existing expectations modulating sensory experience, potentially resulting in the generation of hallucinations. Hallucinations are associated with less accurate sensory processing, and more intense stimuli with less interference are necessary for accurate processing and the appearance of gamma oscillations (called "gamma synchrony"). Hallucinations are also associated with the absence of reduction in P50 amplitude in response to the presentation of a second stimuli after an initial stimulus; this is thought to represent failure to gate sensory stimuli, and can be exacerbated by [dopamine](/wiki/Dopamine "Dopamine") release agents.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

Abnormal assignment of salience to stimuli may be one mechanism of hallucinations. Dysfunctional dopamine signaling may lead to abnormal top down regulation of sensory processing, allowing expectations to distort sensory input.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

## Treatments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Treatments")]

There are few treatments for many types of hallucinations. However, for those hallucinations caused by mental disease, a psychologist or psychiatrist should be consulted, and treatment will be based on the observations of those doctors. [Antipsychotic](/wiki/Antipsychotic "Antipsychotic") and [atypical antipsychotic](/wiki/Atypical_antipsychotic "Atypical antipsychotic") medication may also be utilized to treat the illness if the symptoms are severe and cause significant distress.[[89]](#cite_note-89) For other causes of hallucinations there is no factual evidence to support any one treatment is scientifically tested and proven. However, abstaining from [hallucinogenic](/wiki/Hallucinogen "Hallucinogen") drugs, stimulant drugs, managing stress levels, living healthily, and getting plenty of sleep can help reduce the prevalence of hallucinations. In all cases of hallucinations, medical attention should be sought out and informed of one's specific symptoms. [Meta-analyses](/wiki/Meta-analysis "Meta-analysis") show that [cognitive behavioral therapy](/wiki/Cognitive_behavioral_therapy "Cognitive behavioral therapy")[[4]](#cite_note-What_Constitutes_Sufficient_Evidenc-4) and [metacognitive training](/wiki/Metacognitive_training "Metacognitive training")[[5]](#cite_note-Immediate_and_Sustained_Outcomes_an-5) can also reduce the severity of hallucinations. Furthermore, there are recovery movements all around the world that advocate for individuals with schizophrenia or voice-hearers (individuals that hear voices). The [Hearing Voices Movement](/wiki/Hearing_Voices_Movement "Hearing Voices Movement"),[[90]](#cite_note-90)[*[circular reference](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Wikipedia_and_sources_that_mirror_or_use_it "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] starting in Europe, aims to[*[neutrality](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view "Wikipedia:Neutral point of view") is [disputed](/wiki/Talk:Hallucination "Talk:Hallucination")*] utilize knowledge and experience of voice hearers combined with experts in disorders such as schizophrenia, such as psychiatrists.

## Epidemiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Epidemiology")]

Prevalence of hallucinations varies depending on underlying medical conditions,[[91]](#cite_note-:2-91)[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) which sensory modalities are affected,[[10]](#cite_note-:1-10) age[[92]](#cite_note-:3-92)[[91]](#cite_note-:2-91) and culture.[[93]](#cite_note-93) As of 2022,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Hallucination&action=edit) auditory hallucinations are the most well studied and most common sensory modality of hallucinations, with an estimated lifetime prevalence of 9.6%.[[92]](#cite_note-:3-92) Children and adolescents have been found to experience similar rates (12.7% and 12.4% respectively) which occur mostly during late childhood and adolescence. In this group, hallucinations are not necessarily indicative of later psychopathology and are recognized to occur on a continuum which includes normal, transient hallucinatory phenomena.[[94]](#cite_note-:11-94) However, hallucinations become increasingly associated with psychopathology in late adolescence.[[94]](#cite_note-:11-94)

The prevalence of hallucinations in adults and those over 60 is comparatively lower (with rates of 5.8% and 4.8% respectively).[[92]](#cite_note-:3-92)[[91]](#cite_note-:2-91) For those with schizophrenia, the lifetime prevalence of hallucinations is 80%[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) and the estimated prevalence of visual hallucinations is 27%, compared to 79% for auditory hallucinations.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) A 2019 study suggested 16.2% of adults with [hearing impairment](/wiki/Hearing_loss "Hearing loss") experience hallucinations, with prevalence rising to 24% in the most hearing impaired group.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

A risk factor for multimodal hallucinations is prior experience of unimodal hallucinations.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) In 90% cases of psychosis, a visual hallucination occurs in combination with another sensory modality, most often being auditory or somatic.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9) In schizophrenia, multimodal hallucinations are twice as common as unimodal ones.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9)

A 2015 review of 55 publications from 1962 to 2014 found 16–28.6% of those experiencing hallucinations report at least some religious content in them,[[96]](#cite_note-:4-96): 415 along with 20–60% reporting [some religious content in delusions](/wiki/Religious_delusion "Religious delusion").[[96]](#cite_note-:4-96): 415 There is some evidence for [delusions](/wiki/Delusion "Delusion") being a risk factor for religious hallucinations, with and 61.7% of people having experienced any delusion and 75.9% of those having experienced a religious delusion found to also experience hallucinations.[[96]](#cite_note-:4-96): 421
